# Voice & Tone Policy

This policy defines how we write customer-facing content — changelog posts,
roadmap items, announcements, and any other public communications. The GTM
workflow reads this file at runtime. Edit it to evolve the brand voice.

## Our Voice

We sound like a **knowledgeable friend** — someone who's genuinely excited
about the product and respects the reader's time. We're clear, warm, and
confident without being pushy or corporate.

### Core Principles

1. **Be human, not corporate.** Write like you're explaining something to a
   smart colleague over coffee. Avoid jargon, buzzwords, and hollow phrases
   like "leverage synergies" or "best-in-class solution."

2. **Lead with the benefit.** Don't start with what we built — start with
   what the reader can now do. "You can now export your data in one click"
   beats "We've implemented a new data export feature."

3. **Be specific, not vague.** Concrete details build trust. "Exports complete
   in under 30 seconds" is better than "blazing fast performance."

4. **Keep it brief.** Respect the reader's time. If a sentence doesn't add
   value, cut it. If a paragraph can be a sentence, shrink it.

5. **Celebrate without overselling.** It's fine to be excited about what we've
   shipped. It's not fine to call everything "revolutionary" or "game-changing."

## Tone by Content Type

### Changelog Posts

- **Tone:** Friendly, informative, concise
- **Length:** 2-4 short paragraphs max
- **Structure:**
  - One-line summary of what changed (the headline)
  - What you can now do / what's different (1-2 paragraphs)
  - Any action needed ("no action required" or "update your settings")
  - Link to docs for details
- **Emoji:** Use sparingly — one per section header is fine, not in every sentence
- **Example opener:** "You can now export your account data as a CSV — right
  from your settings page."

### Public Roadmap Items

- **Tone:** Transparent, forward-looking, grounded
- **Length:** 1-2 paragraphs + a brief scope list
- **Structure:**
  - What we're building and why (1 paragraph)
  - Who it's for (1 sentence)
  - What's included at a high level (bullet list, 3-5 items)
  - Current status and expected timeline (phase + target date)
- **Avoid:** Promising specific dates as commitments, using "coming soon"
  without context, over-scoping what's planned
- **Example opener:** "We're adding support for EU payment methods so
  customers in Europe can pay with the options they expect."

## Formatting Guidelines

- Use **sentence case** for headings (not Title Case)
- Use **active voice** ("We added" not "It was added")
- Use **present tense** for changelog ("You can now..." not "You will be able to...")
- Use **future tense** for roadmap ("We're building..." or "This will include...")
- Keep paragraphs to 3 sentences max
- Use bullet lists for multiple items — don't bury them in prose

## Words We Use / Avoid

| ✅ Use | ❌ Avoid |
|--------|---------|
| You can now... | We are pleased to announce... |
| We fixed a bug where... | An issue has been resolved... |
| This works with... | This seamlessly integrates with... |
| Faster | Blazing fast / lightning fast |
| New | All-new / brand-new / revolutionary |
| Simple | Effortless / frictionless |
| Check out the docs | Please refer to the documentation |
| Let us know | Don't hesitate to reach out |

## Audience

Write for **practitioners** — the people who actually use the product day to
day. They're smart, busy, and want to know what changed and whether they need
to do anything. They don't need to be impressed; they need to be informed.

## Updates

This is a living document. If the voice feels off, open a PR. The goal is
consistency, not rigidity.
